name: wheel
documentation:
# wheel

This is a command line tool for manipulating Python wheel files, as defined in
[PEP 427]. It contains the following functionality:

- Convert `.egg` archives into `.whl`
- Unpack wheel archives
- Repack wheel archives
- Add or remove tags in existing wheel archives

## Historical note

This project used to contain the implementation of the [setuptools] `bdist_wheel`
command, but as of setuptools v70.1, it no longer needs `wheel` installed for that to
work. Thus, you should install this **only** if you intend to use the `wheel` command
line tool!

## Documentation

The [documentation] can be found on Read The Docs.

## Code of Conduct

Everyone interacting in the wheel project's codebases, issue trackers, chat
rooms, and mailing lists is expected to follow the [PSF Code of Conduct].

[documentation]: https://wheel.readthedocs.io/
[pep 427]: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0427/
[psf code of conduct]: https://github.com/pypa/.github/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md
[setuptools]: https://pypi.org/project/setuptools/

functions.name: wheel.util.urlsafe_b64encode

functions.name: wheel.util.urlsafe_b64decode

functions.name: wheel.cli.unpack.unpack
documentation:
Unpack a wheel.

Wheel content will be unpacked to \{dest}/\{name}-\{ver}, where \{name}
is the package name and \{ver} its version.

```
param path

The path to the wheel.

param dest

Destination directory (default to current directory).
```

If so how do we do that? Do we need to test against the _name_ of

functions.name: wheel.cli.pack.pack
documentation:
Repack a previously unpacked wheel directory into a new wheel file.

The .dist-info/WHEEL file must contain one or more tags so that the target
wheel file name can be determined.

```
param directory

The unpacked wheel directory

param dest_dir

Destination directory (defaults to the current directory)
```

Compute a tagline from a list of tags.

```
param tags

A list of tags

return

A tagline
```

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._tokenizer.ParserSyntaxError
documentation: The provided source text could not be parsed correctly.

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._tokenizer.Tokenizer

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._tokenizer.Tokenizer.consume
documentation: Move beyond provided token name, if at current position.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._tokenizer.Tokenizer.check
documentation:
Check whether the next token has the provided name.

By default, if the check succeeds, the token *must* be read before
another check. If `peek` is set to `True`, the token is not loaded and
would need to be checked again.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._tokenizer.Tokenizer.expect
documentation:
Expect a certain token name next, failing with a syntax error otherwise.

The token is *not* read.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._tokenizer.Tokenizer.read
documentation: Consume the next token and return it.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._tokenizer.Tokenizer.raise_syntax_error
documentation: Raise ParserSyntaxError at the given position.

Context-sensitive token parsing.

Provides methods to examine the input stream to check whether the next token
matches.

modules.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._elffile
documentation:
ELF file parser.

This provides a class `ELFFile` that parses an ELF executable in a similar
interface to `ZipFile`. Only the read interface is implemented.

Based on: <https://gist.github.com/lyssdod/f51579ae8d93c8657a5564aefc2ffbca>
ELF header: <https://refspecs.linuxfoundation.org/elf/gabi4+/ch4.eheader.html>

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._elffile.ELFFile

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._elffile.ELFFile.interpreter
documentation: The path recorded in the `PT_INTERP` section header.
Representation of an ELF executable.

modules.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._musllinux
documentation:
PEP 656 support.

This module implements logic to detect if the currently running Python is
linked against musl, and what musl version is used.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._musllinux._get_musl_version
documentation:
Detect currently-running musl runtime version.

This is done by checking the specified executable's dynamic linking
information, and invoking the loader to parse its output for a version
string. If the loader is musl, the output would be something like:

```
musl libc (x86_64)
Version 1.2.2
Dynamic Program Loader
```

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._musllinux.platform_tags
documentation:
Generate musllinux tags compatible to the current platform.

```
param archs

Sequence of compatible architectures.
The first one shall be the closest to the actual architecture and be the part of
platform tag after the linux_ prefix, e.g. x86_64.
The linux_ prefix is assumed as a prerequisite for the current platform to
be musllinux-compatible.

returns

An iterator of compatible musllinux tags.
```

functions.name: wheel.cli.tags._compute_tags
documentation: Add or replace tags. Supports dot-separated tags

functions.name: wheel.cli.tags.tags
documentation:
Change the tags on a wheel file.

The tags are left unchanged if they are not specified. To specify "none",
use \["none"\]. To append to the previous tags, a tag should start with a
"+".  If a tag starts with "-", it will be removed from existing tags.
Processing is done left to right.

```
param wheel

The paths to the wheels

param python_tags

The Python tags to set

param abi_tags

The ABI tags to set

param platform_tags

The platform tags to set

param build_tag

The build tag to set

param remove

Remove the original wheel
```

modules.name: wheel.metadata
documentation: Tools for converting old- to new-style metadata.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.yield_lines
documentation:
Yield valid lines of a string or iterable.
\>>> list(yield_lines(''))
\[\]
\>>> list(yield_lines(\['foo', 'bar'\]))
\['foo', 'bar'\]
\>>> list(yield_lines('foonbar'))
\['foo', 'bar'\]
\>>> list(yield_lines('nfoon#barnbaz #comment'))
\['foo', 'baz #comment'\]
\>>> list(yield_lines(\['foonbar', 'baz', 'bingnnn'\]))
\['foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'bing'\]

functions.name: wheel.metadata.split_sections
documentation:
Split a string or iterable thereof into (section, content) pairs
Each `section` is a stripped version of the section header ("\[section\]")
and each `content` is a list of stripped lines excluding blank lines and
comment-only lines.  If there are any such lines before the first section
header, they're returned in a first `section` of `None`.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.safe_extra
documentation:
Convert an arbitrary string to a standard 'extra' name
Any runs of non-alphanumeric characters are replaced with a single '_',
and the result is always lowercased.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.safe_name
documentation:
Convert an arbitrary string to a standard distribution name
Any runs of non-alphanumeric/. characters are replaced with a single '-'.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.requires_to_requires_dist
documentation: Return the version specifier for a requirement in PEP 345/566 fashion.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.convert_requirements
documentation: Yield Requires-Dist: strings for parsed requirements strings.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.generate_requirements
documentation:
Convert requirements from a setup()-style dictionary to
('Requires-Dist', 'requirement') and ('Provides-Extra', 'extra') tuples.

extras_require is a dictionary of {extra: [requirements]} as passed to setup(),
using the empty extra {'': [requirements]} to hold install_requires.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.pkginfo_to_metadata
documentation: Convert .egg-info directory with PKG-INFO to the Metadata 2.1 format

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._manylinux._parse_glibc_version
documentation:
Parse glibc version.

We use a regexp instead of str.split because we want to discard any
random junk that might come after the minor version -- this might happen
in patched/forked versions of glibc (e.g. Linaro's version of glibc
uses version strings like "2.20-2014.11"). See gh-3588.

```
param archs

Sequence of compatible architectures.
The first one shall be the closest to the actual architecture and be the part of
platform tag after the linux_ prefix, e.g. x86_64.
The linux_ prefix is assumed as a prerequisite for the current platform to
be manylinux-compatible.

```

modules.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser
documentation:
Handwritten parser of dependency specifiers.

The docstring for each __parse_* function contains EBNF-inspired grammar representing
the implementation.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser.parse_requirement
documentation: Recursive descent parser for dependency specifier

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_requirement
documentation: requirement = WS? IDENTIFIER WS? extras WS? requirement_details

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_requirement_details
documentation:
requirement_details = AT URL (WS requirement_marker?)?
                    | specifier WS? (requirement_marker)?

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_requirement_marker
documentation: requirement_marker = SEMICOLON marker WS?

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_extras
documentation: extras = (LEFT_BRACKET wsp* extras_list? wsp* RIGHT_BRACKET)?

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_extras_list
documentation: extras_list = identifier (wsp* ',' wsp* identifier)*

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_specifier
documentation:
specifier = LEFT_PARENTHESIS WS? version_many WS? RIGHT_PARENTHESIS
          | WS? version_many WS?

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_version_many
documentation: version_many = (SPECIFIER (WS? COMMA WS? SPECIFIER)*)?

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser.parse_marker
documentation: Recursive descent parser for marker expression

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_marker
documentation: marker = marker_atom (BOOLOP marker_atom)+

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_marker_atom
documentation:
marker_atom = WS? LEFT_PARENTHESIS WS? marker WS? RIGHT_PARENTHESIS WS?
            | WS? marker_item WS?

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_marker_item
documentation: marker_item = WS? marker_var WS? marker_op WS? marker_var WS?

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_marker_var
documentation: marker_var = VARIABLE | QUOTED_STRING

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._parser._parse_marker_op
documentation: marker_op = IN | NOT IN | OP

classes.name: wheel.wheelfile.WheelFile
documentation:
A ZipFile derivative class that also reads SHA-256 hashes from
.dist-info/RECORD and checks any read files against those.

modules.name: wheel.macosx_libfile
documentation:
This module contains function to analyse dynamic library
headers to extract system information

Currently only for MacOSX

Library file on macosx system starts with Mach-O or Fat field.
This can be distinguish by first 32 bites and it is called magic number.
Proper value of magic number is with suffix _MAGIC. Suffix _CIGAM means
reversed bytes order.
Both fields can occur in two types: 32 and 64 bytes.

FAT field inform that this library contains few version of library
(typically for different types version). It contains
information where Mach-O headers starts.

Each section started with Mach-O header contains one library
(So if file starts with this field it contains only one version).

After filed Mach-O there are section fields.
Each of them starts with two fields:
cmd - magic number for this command
cmdsize - total size occupied by this section information.

In this case only sections LC_VERSION_MIN_MACOSX (for macosx 10.13 and earlier)
and LC_BUILD_VERSION (for macosx 10.14 and newer) are interesting,
because them contains information about minimal system version.

Important remarks:
- For fat files this implementation looks for maximum number version.
  It not check if it is 32 or 64 and do not compare it with currently built package.
  So it is possible to false report higher version that needed.
- All structures signatures are taken form macosx header files.
- I think that binary format will be more stable than `otool` output.
  and if apple introduce some changes both implementation will need to be updated.
- The system compile will set the deployment target no lower than
  11.0 for arm64 builds. For "Universal 2" builds use the x86_64 deployment
  target when the arm64 target is 11.0.

functions.name: wheel.macosx_libfile.read_mach_header
documentation:
This function parses a Mach-O header and extracts
information about the minimal macOS version.

```
param lib_file

reference to opened library file with pointer
```

functions.name: wheel.macosx_libfile.calculate_macosx_platform_tag
documentation:
Calculate proper macosx platform tag basing on files which are included to wheel

Example platform tag `macosx-10.14-x86_64`

variables.name: wheel.macosx_libfile.mach_header_fields
wheel.macosx_libfile.mach_header_fields_64
wheel.macosx_libfile.fat_header_fields
wheel.macosx_libfile.fat_arch_fields
wheel.macosx_libfile.fat_arch_64_fields
wheel.macosx_libfile.segment_base_fields
documentation: base for reading segment info

variables.name: wheel.macosx_libfile.segment_command_fields
documentation:
struct segment_command { /* for 32-bit architectures */
    uint32_t    cmd;            /* LC_SEGMENT */
    uint32_t    cmdsize;        /* includes sizeof section structs */
    char                segname[16];    /* segment name */
    uint32_t    vmaddr;         /* memory address of this segment */
    uint32_t    vmsize;         /* memory size of this segment */
    uint32_t    fileoff;        /* file offset of this segment */
    uint32_t    filesize;       /* amount to map from the file */
    vm_prot_t   maxprot;        /* maximum VM protection */
    vm_prot_t   initprot;       /* initial VM protection */
    uint32_t    nsects;         /* number of sections in segment */
    uint32_t    flags;          /* flags */
};
typedef int vm_prot_t;

variables.name: wheel.macosx_libfile.segment_command_fields_64
documentation:
struct segment_command_64 { /* for 64-bit architectures */
    uint32_t    cmd;            /* LC_SEGMENT_64 */
    uint32_t    cmdsize;        /* includes sizeof section_64 structs */
    char                segname[16];    /* segment name */
    uint64_t    vmaddr;         /* memory address of this segment */
    uint64_t    vmsize;         /* memory size of this segment */
    uint64_t    fileoff;        /* file offset of this segment */
    uint64_t    filesize;       /* amount to map from the file */
    vm_prot_t   maxprot;        /* maximum VM protection */
    vm_prot_t   initprot;       /* initial VM protection */
    uint32_t    nsects;         /* number of sections in segment */
    uint32_t    flags;          /* flags */
};

variables.name: wheel.macosx_libfile.version_min_command_fields
wheel.macosx_libfile.build_version_command_fields

modules.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags
documentation: This file is dual licensed under the terms of the Apache License, Version
2.0, and the BSD License. See the LICENSE file in the root of this repository
for complete details.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.parse_tag
documentation:
Parses the provided tag (e.g. `py3-none-any`) into a frozenset of Tag instances.

Returning a set is required due to the possibility that the tag is a
compressed tag set.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._is_threaded_cpython
documentation:
Determine if the ABI corresponds to a threaded (`--disable-gil`) build.

The threaded builds are indicated by a "t" in the abiflags.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._abi3_applies
documentation:
Determine if the Python version supports abi3.

PEP 384 was first implemented in Python 3.2. The threaded (`--disable-gil`)
builds do not support abi3.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.cpython_tags
documentation:
Yields the tags for a CPython interpreter.

The tags consist of:
- cp<python_version>-<abi>-<platform>
- cp<python_version>-abi3-<platform>
- cp<python_version>-none-<platform>
- cp<less than python_version>-abi3-<platform>  # Older Python versions down to 3.2.

If python_version only specifies a major version then user-provided ABIs and
the 'none' ABItag will be used.

If 'abi3' or 'none' are specified in 'abis' then they will be yielded at
their normal position and not at the beginning.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._generic_abi
documentation: Return the ABI tag based on EXT_SUFFIX.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.generic_tags
documentation:
Yields the tags for a generic interpreter.

The tags consist of:
- <interpreter>-<abi>-<platform>

The "none" ABI will be added if it was not explicitly provided.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._py_interpreter_range
documentation:
Yields Python versions in descending order.

After the latest version, the major-only version will be yielded, and then
all previous versions of that major version.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.compatible_tags
documentation:
Yields the sequence of tags that are compatible with a specific version of Python.

The tags consist of:
- py*-none-<platform>
- <interpreter>-none-any  # ... if `interpreter` is provided.
- py*-none-any

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.mac_platforms
documentation:
Yields the platform tags for a macOS system.

The `version` parameter is a two-item tuple specifying the macOS version to
generate platform tags for. The `arch` parameter is the CPU architecture to
generate platform tags for. Both parameters default to the appropriate value
for the current system.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.platform_tags
documentation: Provides the platform tags for this installation.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.interpreter_name
documentation:
Returns the name of the running interpreter.

Some implementations have a reserved, two-letter abbreviation which will
be returned when appropriate.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.interpreter_version
documentation: Returns the version of the running interpreter.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.sys_tags
documentation:
Returns the sequence of tag triples for the running interpreter.

The order of the sequence corresponds to priority order for the
interpreter, from most to least important.

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.Tag
documentation:
A representation of the tag triple for a wheel.

Instances are considered immutable and thus are hashable. Equality checking
is also supported.

modules.name: wheel._bdist_wheel

Create a wheel (.whl) distribution.

A wheel is a built archive format.

functions.name: wheel._bdist_wheel.safe_name

Convert an arbitrary string to a standard distribution name
Any runs of non-alphanumeric/. characters are replaced with a single '-'.

functions.name: wheel._bdist_wheel.safe_version

functions.name: wheel._bdist_wheel.get_platform

functions.name: wheel._bdist_wheel.get_flag
documentation:
Use a fallback value for determining SOABI flags if the needed config
var is unset or unavailable.

functions.name: wheel._bdist_wheel.get_abi_tag

methods.name: wheel._bdist_wheel.bdist_wheel.wheel_dist_name
documentation: Return distribution full name with - replaced with _

```
    from packaging.version import parse, Version
```

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.parse
documentation:
Parse the given version string.

```pycon
>>> parse('1.0.dev1')
<Version('1.0.dev1')>
```

```
param version

The version string to parse.

raises InvalidVersion

When the version string is not a valid version.
```

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version._parse_local_version
documentation: Takes a string like abc.1.twelve and turns it into ("abc", 1, "twelve").

variables.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.VERSION_PATTERN
documentation:
A string containing the regular expression used to match a valid version.

The pattern is not anchored at either end, and is intended for embedding in larger
expressions (for example, matching a version number as part of a file name). The
regular expression should be compiled with the `re.VERBOSE` and `re.IGNORECASE`
flags set.

```
meta hide-value

```

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.InvalidVersion
documentation:
Raised when a version string is not a valid version.

```pycon
>>> Version("invalid")
Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
packaging.version.InvalidVersion: Invalid version: 'invalid'
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version._BaseVersion.__lt__
documentation: Please keep the duplicated `isinstance` check
in the six comparisons hereunder
unless you find a way to avoid adding overhead function calls.

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.__init__
documentation:
Initialize a Version object.

```
param version

The string representation of a version which will be parsed and normalized
before use.

raises InvalidVersion

If the version does not conform to PEP 440 in any way then this
exception will be raised.
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.__repr__
documentation:
A representation of the Version that shows all internal state.

```pycon
>>> Version('1.0.0')
<Version('1.0.0')>
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.__str__
documentation:
A string representation of the version that can be rounded-tripped.

```pycon
>>> str(Version("1.0a5"))
'1.0a5'
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.epoch
documentation:
The epoch of the version.

```pycon
>>> Version("2.0.0").epoch
0
>>> Version("1!2.0.0").epoch
1
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.release
documentation:
The components of the "release" segment of the version.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").release
(1, 2, 3)
>>> Version("2.0.0").release
(2, 0, 0)
>>> Version("1!2.0.0.post0").release
(2, 0, 0)
```

Includes trailing zeroes but not the epoch or any pre-release / development /
post-release suffixes.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.pre
documentation:
The pre-release segment of the version.

```pycon
>>> print(Version("1.2.3").pre)
None
>>> Version("1.2.3a1").pre
('a', 1)
>>> Version("1.2.3b1").pre
('b', 1)
>>> Version("1.2.3rc1").pre
('rc', 1)
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.post
documentation:
The post-release number of the version.

```pycon
>>> print(Version("1.2.3").post)
None
>>> Version("1.2.3.post1").post
1
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.dev
documentation:
The development number of the version.

```pycon
>>> print(Version("1.2.3").dev)
None
>>> Version("1.2.3.dev1").dev
1
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.local
documentation:
The local version segment of the version.

```pycon
>>> print(Version("1.2.3").local)
None
>>> Version("1.2.3+abc").local
'abc'
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.public
documentation:
The public portion of the version.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").public
'1.2.3'
>>> Version("1.2.3+abc").public
'1.2.3'
>>> Version("1.2.3+abc.dev1").public
'1.2.3'
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.base_version
documentation:
The "base version" of the version.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").base_version
'1.2.3'
>>> Version("1.2.3+abc").base_version
'1.2.3'
>>> Version("1!1.2.3+abc.dev1").base_version
'1!1.2.3'
```

The "base version" is the public version of the project without any pre or post
release markers.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.is_prerelease
documentation:
Whether this version is a pre-release.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").is_prerelease
False
>>> Version("1.2.3a1").is_prerelease
True
>>> Version("1.2.3b1").is_prerelease
True
>>> Version("1.2.3rc1").is_prerelease
True
>>> Version("1.2.3dev1").is_prerelease
True
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.is_postrelease
documentation:
Whether this version is a post-release.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").is_postrelease
False
>>> Version("1.2.3.post1").is_postrelease
True
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.is_devrelease
documentation:
Whether this version is a development release.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").is_devrelease
False
>>> Version("1.2.3.dev1").is_devrelease
True
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.major
documentation:
The first item of {attr}`release` or `0` if unavailable.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").major
1
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.minor
documentation:
The second item of {attr}`release` or `0` if unavailable.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").minor
2
>>> Version("1").minor
0
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.version.Version.micro
documentation:
The third item of {attr}`release` or `0` if unavailable.

```pycon
>>> Version("1.2.3").micro
3
>>> Version("1").micro
0
```

This class abstracts handling of a project's versions.

A {class}`Version` instance is comparison aware and can be compared and
sorted using the standard Python interfaces.

```pycon
>>> v1 = Version("1.0a5")
>>> v2 = Version("1.0")
>>> v1
<Version('1.0a5')>
>>> v2
<Version('1.0')>
>>> v1 < v2
True
>>> v1 == v2
False
>>> v1 > v2
False
>>> v1 >= v2
False
>>> v1 <= v2
True
```

classes.name: wheel.cli.convert.WininstFileSource
documentation:
Handles distributions created with `bdist_wininst`.

The egginfo filename has the format:

```
name-ver(-pyver)(-arch).egg-info
```

The installer filename has the format:

```
name-ver.arch(-pyver).exe
```

Some things to note:

1. The installer filename is not definitive. An installer can be renamed
   and work perfectly well as an installer. So more reliable data should
   be used whenever possible.
2. The egg-info data should be preferred for the name and version, because
   these come straight from the distutils metadata, and are mandatory.
3. The pyver from the egg-info data should be ignored, as it is
   constructed from the version of Python used to build the installer,
   which is irrelevant - the installer filename is correct here (even to
   the point that when it's not there, any version is implied).
4. The architecture must be taken from the installer filename, as it is
   not included in the egg-info data.
5. Architecture-neutral installers still have an architecture because the
   installer format itself (being executable) is architecture-specific. We
   should therefore ignore the architecture if the content is pure-python.

modules.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers
documentation:
```
.. testsetup::

    from packaging.specifiers import Specifier, SpecifierSet, InvalidSpecifier
    from packaging.version import Version
```

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers._version_split
documentation:
Split version into components.

The split components are intended for version comparison. The logic does
not attempt to retain the original version string, so joining the
components back with {func}`_version_join` may not produce the original
version string.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers._version_join
documentation:
Join split version components into a version string.

This function assumes the input came from {func}`_version_split`, where the
first component must be the epoch (either empty or numeric), and all other
components numeric.

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.InvalidSpecifier
documentation:
Raised when attempting to create a {class}`Specifier` with a specifier
string that is invalid.

```pycon
>>> Specifier("lolwat")
Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
packaging.specifiers.InvalidSpecifier: Invalid specifier: 'lolwat'
```

documentation:
Returns the str representation of this Specifier-like object. This
should be representative of the Specifier itself.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.BaseSpecifier.__hash__
documentation: Returns a hash value for this Specifier-like object.

documentation:
Returns a boolean representing whether or not the two Specifier-like
objects are equal.

```
param other

The other object to check against.
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.BaseSpecifier.prereleases
documentation:
Whether or not pre-releases as a whole are allowed.

This can be set to either `True` or `False` to explicitly enable or disable
prereleases or it can be set to `None` (the default) to use default semantics.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.BaseSpecifier.prereleases
documentation:
Setter for {attr}`prereleases`.

```
param value

The value to set.
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.BaseSpecifier.contains
documentation: Determines if the given item is contained within this specifier.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.BaseSpecifier.filter
documentation:
Takes an iterable of items and filters them so that only items which
are contained within this specifier are allowed in it.

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.__init__
documentation:
Initialize a Specifier instance.

```
param spec

The string representation of a specifier which will be parsed and
normalized before use.

param prereleases

This tells the specifier if it should accept prerelease versions if
applicable or not. The default of None will autodetect it from the
given specifiers.

raises InvalidSpecifier

If the given specifier is invalid (i.e. bad syntax).
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.prereleases
documentation: https://github.com/python/mypy/pull/13475#pullrequestreview-1079784515

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.operator
documentation:
The operator of this specifier.

```pycon
>>> Specifier("==1.2.3").operator
'=='
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.version
documentation:
The version of this specifier.

```pycon
>>> Specifier("==1.2.3").version
'1.2.3'
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.__repr__
documentation:
A representation of the Specifier that shows all internal state.

```pycon
>>> Specifier('>=1.0.0')
<Specifier('>=1.0.0')>
>>> Specifier('>=1.0.0', prereleases=False)
<Specifier('>=1.0.0', prereleases=False)>
>>> Specifier('>=1.0.0', prereleases=True)
<Specifier('>=1.0.0', prereleases=True)>
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.__str__
documentation:
A string representation of the Specifier that can be round-tripped.

```pycon
>>> str(Specifier('>=1.0.0'))
'>=1.0.0'
>>> str(Specifier('>=1.0.0', prereleases=False))
'>=1.0.0'
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.__eq__
documentation:
Whether or not the two Specifier-like objects are equal.

```
param other

The other object to check against.
```

The value of {attr}`prereleases` is ignored.

```pycon
>>> Specifier("==1.2.3") == Specifier("== 1.2.3.0")
True
>>> (Specifier("==1.2.3", prereleases=False) ==
...  Specifier("==1.2.3", prereleases=True))
True
>>> Specifier("==1.2.3") == "==1.2.3"
True
>>> Specifier("==1.2.3") == Specifier("==1.2.4")
False
>>> Specifier("==1.2.3") == Specifier("~=1.2.3")
False
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.__contains__
documentation:
Return whether or not the item is contained in this specifier.

```
param item

The item to check for.
```

This is used for the `in` operator and behaves the same as
{meth}`contains` with no `prereleases` argument passed.

```pycon
>>> "1.2.3" in Specifier(">=1.2.3")
True
>>> Version("1.2.3") in Specifier(">=1.2.3")
True
>>> "1.0.0" in Specifier(">=1.2.3")
False
>>> "1.3.0a1" in Specifier(">=1.2.3")
False
>>> "1.3.0a1" in Specifier(">=1.2.3", prereleases=True)
True
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.contains
documentation:
Return whether or not the item is contained in this specifier.

```
param item

The item to check for, which can be a version string or a
 instance.

param prereleases

Whether or not to match prereleases with this Specifier. If set to
None (the default), it uses  to determine
whether or not prereleases are allowed.
```

```pycon
>>> Specifier(">=1.2.3").contains("1.2.3")
True
>>> Specifier(">=1.2.3").contains(Version("1.2.3"))
True
>>> Specifier(">=1.2.3").contains("1.0.0")
False
>>> Specifier(">=1.2.3").contains("1.3.0a1")
False
>>> Specifier(">=1.2.3", prereleases=True).contains("1.3.0a1")
True
>>> Specifier(">=1.2.3").contains("1.3.0a1", prereleases=True)
True
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.Specifier.filter
documentation:
Filter items in the given iterable, that match the specifier.

```
param iterable

An iterable that can contain version strings and  instances.
The items in the iterable will be filtered according to the specifier.

param prereleases

Whether or not to allow prereleases in the returned iterator. If set to
None (the default), it will be intelligently decide whether to allow
prereleases or not (based on the  attribute, and
whether the only versions matching are prereleases).
```

This method is smarter than just `filter(Specifier().contains, [...])`
because it implements the rule from {pep}`440` that a prerelease item
SHOULD be accepted if no other versions match the given specifier.

```pycon
>>> list(Specifier(">=1.2.3").filter(["1.2", "1.3", "1.5a1"]))
['1.3']
>>> list(Specifier(">=1.2.3").filter(["1.2", "1.2.3", "1.3", Version("1.4")]))
['1.2.3', '1.3', <Version('1.4')>]
>>> list(Specifier(">=1.2.3").filter(["1.2", "1.5a1"]))
['1.5a1']
>>> list(Specifier(">=1.2.3").filter(["1.3", "1.5a1"], prereleases=True))
['1.3', '1.5a1']
>>> list(Specifier(">=1.2.3", prereleases=True).filter(["1.3", "1.5a1"]))
['1.3', '1.5a1']
```

This class abstracts handling of version specifiers.

:::{tip}
It is generally not required to instantiate this manually. You should instead
prefer to work with {class}`SpecifierSet` instead, which can parse
comma-separated version specifiers (which is what package metadata contains).
:::

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.__init__
documentation:
Initialize a SpecifierSet instance.

```
param specifiers

The string representation of a specifier or a comma-separated list of
specifiers which will be parsed and normalized before use.

param prereleases

This tells the SpecifierSet if it should accept prerelease versions if
applicable or not. The default of None will autodetect it from the
given specifiers.

raises InvalidSpecifier

If the given specifiers are not parseable than this exception will be
raised.
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.__repr__
documentation:
A representation of the specifier set that shows all internal state.

Note that the ordering of the individual specifiers within the set may not
match the input string.

```pycon
>>> SpecifierSet('>=1.0.0,!=2.0.0')
<SpecifierSet('!=2.0.0,>=1.0.0')>
>>> SpecifierSet('>=1.0.0,!=2.0.0', prereleases=False)
<SpecifierSet('!=2.0.0,>=1.0.0', prereleases=False)>
>>> SpecifierSet('>=1.0.0,!=2.0.0', prereleases=True)
<SpecifierSet('!=2.0.0,>=1.0.0', prereleases=True)>
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.__str__
documentation:
A string representation of the specifier set that can be round-tripped.

Note that the ordering of the individual specifiers within the set may not
match the input string.

```pycon
>>> str(SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1"))
'!=1.0.1,>=1.0.0'
>>> str(SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1", prereleases=False))
'!=1.0.1,>=1.0.0'
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.__and__
documentation:
Return a SpecifierSet which is a combination of the two sets.

```
param other

The other object to combine with.
```

```pycon
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1") & '<=2.0.0,!=2.0.1'
<SpecifierSet('!=1.0.1,!=2.0.1,<=2.0.0,>=1.0.0')>
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1") & SpecifierSet('<=2.0.0,!=2.0.1')
<SpecifierSet('!=1.0.1,!=2.0.1,<=2.0.0,>=1.0.0')>
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.__eq__
documentation:
Whether or not the two SpecifierSet-like objects are equal.

```
param other

The other object to check against.
```

The value of {attr}`prereleases` is ignored.

```pycon
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1") == SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1")
True
>>> (SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1", prereleases=False) ==
...  SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1", prereleases=True))
True
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1") == ">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1"
True
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1") == SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0")
False
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1") == SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.2")
False
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.__len__
documentation: Returns the number of specifiers in this specifier set.

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.__iter__
documentation:
Returns an iterator over all the underlying {class}`Specifier` instances
in this specifier set.

```pycon
>>> sorted(SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1"), key=str)
[<Specifier('!=1.0.1')>, <Specifier('>=1.0.0')>]
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.__contains__
documentation:
Return whether or not the item is contained in this specifier.

```
param item

The item to check for.
```

This is used for the `in` operator and behaves the same as
{meth}`contains` with no `prereleases` argument passed.

```pycon
>>> "1.2.3" in SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1")
True
>>> Version("1.2.3") in SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1")
True
>>> "1.0.1" in SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1")
False
>>> "1.3.0a1" in SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1")
False
>>> "1.3.0a1" in SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1", prereleases=True)
True
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.contains
documentation:
Return whether or not the item is contained in this SpecifierSet.

```
param item

The item to check for, which can be a version string or a
 instance.

param prereleases

Whether or not to match prereleases with this SpecifierSet. If set to
None (the default), it uses  to determine
whether or not prereleases are allowed.
```

```pycon
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1").contains("1.2.3")
True
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1").contains(Version("1.2.3"))
True
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1").contains("1.0.1")
False
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1").contains("1.3.0a1")
False
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1", prereleases=True).contains("1.3.0a1")
True
>>> SpecifierSet(">=1.0.0,!=1.0.1").contains("1.3.0a1", prereleases=True)
True
```

methods.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.specifiers.SpecifierSet.filter
documentation:
Filter items in the given iterable, that match the specifiers in this set.

```
param iterable

An iterable that can contain version strings and  instances.
The items in the iterable will be filtered according to the specifier.

param prereleases

Whether or not to allow prereleases in the returned iterator. If set to
None (the default), it will be intelligently decide whether to allow
prereleases or not (based on the  attribute, and
whether the only versions matching are prereleases).
```

This method is smarter than just `filter(SpecifierSet(...).contains, [...])`
because it implements the rule from {pep}`440` that a prerelease item
SHOULD be accepted if no other versions match the given specifier.

```pycon
>>> list(SpecifierSet(">=1.2.3").filter(["1.2", "1.3", "1.5a1"]))
['1.3']
>>> list(SpecifierSet(">=1.2.3").filter(["1.2", "1.3", Version("1.4")]))
['1.3', <Version('1.4')>]
>>> list(SpecifierSet(">=1.2.3").filter(["1.2", "1.5a1"]))
[]
>>> list(SpecifierSet(">=1.2.3").filter(["1.3", "1.5a1"], prereleases=True))
['1.3', '1.5a1']
>>> list(SpecifierSet(">=1.2.3", prereleases=True).filter(["1.3", "1.5a1"]))
['1.3', '1.5a1']
```

An "empty" SpecifierSet will filter items based on the presence of prerelease
versions in the set.

```pycon
>>> list(SpecifierSet("").filter(["1.3", "1.5a1"]))
['1.3']
>>> list(SpecifierSet("").filter(["1.5a1"]))
['1.5a1']
>>> list(SpecifierSet("", prereleases=True).filter(["1.3", "1.5a1"]))
['1.3', '1.5a1']
>>> list(SpecifierSet("").filter(["1.3", "1.5a1"], prereleases=True))
['1.3', '1.5a1']
```

This class abstracts handling of a set of version specifiers.

It can be passed a single specifier (`>=3.0`), a comma-separated list of
specifiers (`>=3.0,!=3.1`), or no specifier at all.
